data.title: Thailand rediscovers ‘extinct’ flat-headed cat after 30 years
data.description:
An elusive wild cat long feared extinct in Thailand has been rediscovered three decades after the last recorded sighting, conservation authorities and an NGO said on Friday.
Flat-headed cats are among the world’s rarest and most threatened wild felines. Their range is limited to Southeast Asia and they are endangered because of dwindling habitat.
The domestic cat-sized feline with its distinctive round and close-set eyes was last spotted in a documented sighting in Thailand in 1995.
But an ecological survey that began last year, using camera traps in southern Thailand’s Princess Sirindhorn Wildlife Sanctuary, documented 29 sightings, according to the country’s Department of National Parks, Wildlife and Plant Conservation and wild cat conservation organisation Panthera.
“The rediscovery is exciting, yet concerning at the same time,” said veterinarian and researcher Kaset Sutasha of Kasetsart University, noting that habitat fragmentation has left the species increasingly “isolated”.
It is unclear exactly how many cats were photographed, as the species lacks the markings needed to tell them apart.
But the findings suggest a relatively high concentration of the species, said Panthera conservation programme manager Rattapan Pattanarangsan.
The footage included a female flat-headed cat with her cub – a rare and encouraging sign for a species that typically produces only one offspring at a time.

Nocturnal and elusive, the flat-headed cat typically lives in dense wetland ecosystems such as peat swamps and freshwater mangroves, environments that are extremely difficult for researchers to access, Rattapan said.
Globally, the International Union for Conservation of Nature estimates that around 2,500 adult flat-headed cats remain in the wild, classifying the species as endangered.
In Thailand, it has long been listed as “possibly extinct”.
Thailand’s peat swamp forests have been heavily fragmented, largely due to land conversion and agricultural expansion, said Kaset, who was not involved in the ecological survey but has researched wild cats for years.
The animals also face mounting threats from disease spread by domestic animals, and they struggle to reproduce across isolated areas.
While the rediscovery offers hope, it is only a “starting point” for future conservation efforts, he said.
“What comes after this is more important – how to enable them to live alongside us sustainably, without being threatened.”

data.title: China aircraft carrier allows use of naked flames for TCM therapy which is popular among troops
data.description:
China’s most advanced aircraft carrier, the Fujian, has allowed the use of naked flames on board to accommodate a form of traditional Chinese medicine (TCM).
The use of fire on the vessel is to be allowed so that cupping therapy can take place.
The news has received much attention on mainland social media after it was reported by state broadcaster CCTV on December 24.

The Fujian, China’s third aircraft carrier and the most advanced, officially entered into service in November at a grand ceremony in Sanya, Hainan province.
With a carrying capacity of 1,000 sailors, the Fujian is equipped with the biggest medical centre in the country’s naval fleet.
It is made up of a diagnosis zone, inpatient department, surgery rooms and a physiotherapy room where the TCM treatments moxibustion, cupping and acupuncture take place.
Cupping therapy is an ancient healing technique to ease pain.

The treatment provider first heats the inside of each cup by setting an alcohol-soaked cotton ball aflame, so that heat sends oxygen out of the cup. Then the cups are quickly placed on specific parts of the patient’s body, like the back, stomach, arms and legs, where they stay for some minutes.
The rationale behind the treatment is to use a vacuum or suction force inside the cup to draw blood to or away from specific areas of the body to relieve conditions that cause pain.
According to the military authorities, the flames are “necessary fire”, the report said.

“It plays a definite role in the rehabilitation of sailors and is used in a strictly controlled manner,” a military official was quoted as saying.
The cupping treatment is practised for a short time, on a small scale and by professionals.
Also, the medical centre is installed with fireproof materials and an independent ventilation system.
The use of flames is prohibited in on board kitchens.
Cupping and other TCM physiotherapies are popular among military personnel because soldiers tend to develop problems such as back and neck pain or shoulder stiffness in the wet environment.
The Fujian enforces a strict fire prevention code.
Only electric stoves are used in its kitchen and sailors’ quilts are made of flame-resistant fabrics.

“We attach high importance to fire use on the aircraft carrier because it is seen as ‘a movable ammunition depot’ and we learn lessons from multiple previous fire accidents,” the official said, referring to an incident aboard the US carrier Lexington and Britain’s Dasher decades ago.
“Food can be prepared using electricity, but the health of sailors cannot be ensured with modern technology,” CCTV said in an editorial.
It is not the first time cupping treatment has made international headlines.
American swimming superstar Michael Phelps stunned the public with a raft of marks on his back left by cupping therapy when he took part in the 2016 Olympics.

data.title: China opens shopping centre on Woody Island in disputed Paracels in South China Sea
data.description:
China has opened a new shopping centre on Woody Island in the disputed Paracel Islands, bolstering its civilian presence on the strategically vital South China Sea outpost.
The Sansha City Commercial Centre opened to business on Thursday, according to the local government. The over 6,000 square metre (64,600 sq ft) complex is the latest addition to China’s civilian infrastructure on what it calls Yongxing Island, following its first hardware store last year and a hotpot restaurant in 2023.
Woody Island lies within the Paracel Islands – an archipelago known as the Xisha Islands in Chinese that is at the centre of overlapping territorial claims in the South China Sea.
Woody Island is the largest of around 30 islands in the Paracels chain and serves as the administrative hub of Sansha, a city established in Hainan province in 2012 to assert Beijing’s territorial claims in the South China Sea.

Sansha governs the Paracels, as well as the disputed Spratly Islands and Macclesfield Bank, called Nansha Islands and Zhongsha Islands, respectively, in China.
The city had a permanent population of about 2,200 at the end of 2024, according to data from the Hainan statistics bureau.
China has already built a host of public infrastructure on Woody Island, including a post office, bank, weather station, school, library, hospital, power plant and public parks.
It also has a dual-use civilian and military airport with a 3,000-metre runway capable of accommodating Boeing 737 aircraft, as well as a port able to berth 5,000-tonne vessels.
The new commercial centre combines shopping, dining, cultural and leisure facilities, according to the Sansha government, which described it as a step towards improving the living standards for residents of Woody Island as well as civilian and military personnel stationed there.
The facilities included a supermarket, a fast-food restaurant, a bookstore and a hair salon, officials said.

Beijing’s continued expansion of civilian infrastructure on disputed islands in the South China Sea has prompted concerns from rival claimant Vietnam.
In 2023, Hanoi strongly denounced the opening of the hotpot restaurant on Woody Island, reiterating its position that such activities undermined its claimed sovereignty over the Paracels, which it calls the Hoang Sa Islands.
Beijing claims most South China Sea features and their adjacent waters, but those claims are disputed by neighbours including Vietnam, the Philippines, Malaysia and Brunei.
Over more than a decade, Beijing has steadily consolidated its South China Sea presence through a mix of administrative control, infrastructure development and military deployment, often prompting protests from rival claimants.
In 2016, an international arbitration ruling initiated by the Philippines under the UN Convention on the Law of the Sea invalidated China’s expansive maritime claims as having no legal basis. Beijing boycotted the proceedings and rejected the outcome.

data.title: Shanghai to double down on open-source projects amid push for AI, chip self-sufficiency
data.description:
Shanghai, China’s financial and tech hub, is aiming to foster over 200 open-source projects over the next two years as part of the city’s ambitious road map to build a world-class ecosystem amid the heated AI race with the US.
By 2027, the city would see the establishment of one to two “internationally influential open-source communities”, along with 100 companies focused on related technologies and over 200 high-quality open-source projects, according to an action plan unveiled on Thursday by the Shanghai municipal government.
The total number of developers involved in the open-source communities is expected to reach over three million by that time, it added.
Several frontier sectors – including AI, intelligent chips and high-end software – would become major areas of focus for the initiative. The city also planned to launch China’s first open-source platform targeting overseas markets next year.
The ambitious plan was put forward by a few municipal agencies, led by the Shanghai Municipal Commission of Economy and Informatization, the body in charge of boosting technology development in the city.

The initiative, which aims to make Shanghai “a nationally competitive source and development hub for open-source innovation”, reflects how the city is positioning itself as the vanguard of China’s push for tech self-reliance.
Shanghai is headquarters for a number of AI model developers, including MiniMax and SenseTime, as well as AI chip developers such as Biren Technology and Enflame.
The latest aspirations from Shanghai come as the Chinese government is doubling down on its open-source strategy, especially in AI, to cut reliance on US technologies and foster domestic innovation amid the US-China tech war.
The Shanghai municipal government said it would boost open-source platforms with resources such as pre-trained models, training data sets, development tools and AI applications, to meet the needs of the entire chain from development to training, testing, hosting and operation for AI models.
China’s bet on open-source models has already helped it become a key player in the global AI arena, led by the efforts by start-up DeepSeek and Alibaba Group Holding. Open source allows public access to a software program’s source code, allowing third-party developers to modify or share its design, fix broken links or scale up its capabilities. Alibaba owns the Post.

China’s open-source AI models may have caught up or “even pulled ahead” of their US counterparts in capabilities and adoption, according to a recent report by Stanford University. These “good enough” models with higher degrees of “openness” compared with US models offered practical advantages such as reproducibility and efficiency, especially in less computationally resourced parts of the world, it added.
As part of its open-source plan, Shanghai will also make efforts to push forward the adoption of RISC-V chip architecture, with the goal of building related toolchains covering compilers, simulators and operating system adaptation tools.
RISC-V, the fifth generation of the open-sourced Reduced Instruction Set Computer architecture for processors, is free for anyone to use and modify and has been seen as one of China’s best hopes for achieving chip self-sufficiency.

data.title: Hong Kong Jockey Club plans fourth public riding school in Tseung Kwan O
data.description:
The Hong Kong Jockey Club is planning to build its fourth public riding school on a former landfill site in Tseung Kwan O to reduce applicant wait times and boost the equestrian talent pool.
The project has already gained in-principle support from authorities and the club will seek town planning approval in the first quarter of next year and begin construction as early as the end of 2027, according to a paper submitted to Sai Kung District Council.
The plan will be part of the agenda of a council meeting on January 6.
The facility is expected to be open to the public in 2030 to address strong demand, with more than 20,000 people currently queuing for a spot in the existing three schools and average waiting times lasting several years.
“To cater for the growing demand, the club has decided to open its fourth public riding school in Tseung Kwan O after cautious planning,” the paper read.
“Apart from facilitating residents living in the southeastern New Territories, we also hope to shorten the overall waiting time and nurture more excellent equestrians.
“The proposal can utilise the restored landfill site while promoting Hong Kong’s equestrian sport development. The proposal kills two birds with one stone.”
The club has three public riding schools in Tuen Mun in the New Territories, and Pok Fu Lam and Lei Yue Mun Park on Hong Kong Island.
According to the club’s proposal, the proposed school will take up 46,500 square metres of the former landfill site in Tseung Kwan O.
The school will comprise three low-rise blocks, which will incorporate accessibility design features.
Apart from accommodating equine facilities, such as paddocks and stables, the school will also feature a viewing gallery for the public to look at the training and appreciate the natural scenery.
A pony observation area will be earmarked for the public to get a closer look at the animals and take photos with them, offering a unique experience for visitors.
Upon operation, public open days, regular visits and pony rides will be offered as public education activities.
The club said construction would not involve piling works, while it would also urge contractors to mitigate the impact on nearby residents and the environment.
It added that it would implement a temporary transport management based on the government’s request.
Citing its consultant’s preliminary transport assessment report, the club said the fourth riding school would only slightly increase the district’s vehicle and passenger flow upon operation.
Closed in 1994, the former landfill site in Tseung Kwan O development area 105 has a total of 42 hectares. It is currently used as a training field for unmanned aerial vehicles for land surveying.
In 2005, the site was developed into a model aeroplane training field for the Hong Kong Air Cadet Corps during weekends and public holidays.

data.title: Launch window opens for China’s private space companies as LandSpace advances IPO
data.description:
China’s private rocket makers are stepping up their dash for public funding, with Beijing-based LandSpace becoming the first commercial launch provider to clear a key regulatory “tutoring” milestone ahead of a planned listing on Shanghai’s Nasdaq-style Star Market.
LandSpace completed its initial public offering (IPO) tutoring process on December 23, according to information published on the website of the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) – a step that typically precedes the submission of a formal listing application.
The company is positioning itself as a domestic contender in reusable launch technology, a field dominated by SpaceX.
Investor interest in the sector has also been stirred by reports that Elon Musk’s company is weighing a public offering in 2026 and is targeting a valuation as high as US$1.5 trillion – a figure that, if achieved, would eclipse the world’s largest listings to date.
LandSpace is among roughly 10 Chinese commercial aerospace firms that have moved towards capital markets this year, as the industry tries to secure the deep pools of funding needed for repeated test flights, manufacturing scale-up and eventual reusability.

Several peers – including Galactic Energy, Space Pioneer, i-Space and CAS Space – have filed for IPO tutoring with mainland regulators as they prepare for potential A-share listings, while others have opted for Hong Kong: Adaspace Technology and Fortunetone Technology had submitted applications to the Hong Kong stock exchange.
While SpaceX leads in research, development and implementation of rocket recovery technology, LandSpace has completed two vertical take-off and landing recovery tests and is the world’s first company to send a liquid oxygen methane-powered rocket into orbit.
LandSpace – a darling among venture capital firms – has attracted heavyweight backers in earlier funding rounds.
According to corporate database provider Qichacha, LandSpace had completed more than 10 rounds of fundraising in its early years – raising more than 800 million yuan (US$113 million) in just three years.
In December last year, the National Manufacturing Transformation and Upgrading Fund invested 900 million yuan in LandSpace, making it the company’s largest single investor to date.
Earlier this month, the company carried out the debut flight of its Zhuque-3 rocket from a commercial space test zone in China’s Gansu Province, reaching orbit but failing to recover the first-stage booster – underscoring both the progress and the execution risk inherent in attempts to replicate SpaceX-style reusability.
LandSpace has said it plans further tests as it works towards a successful recovery.
The faster pace of listings is also being helped by policy shifts. In mid-2025, the CSRC expanded Star Market reforms aimed at opening the door wider to hi-tech firms that are not yet profitable – a category that can include capital-intensive sectors such as commercial aerospace.
China’s broader space push is adding momentum, too. The country has logged a near-record number of launches in 2025 – conducting nearly 90 space launches in a year – while policymakers have increasingly framed aerospace as a strategic industry heading into the next five-year planning cycle.
That momentum has yet to translate into SpaceX-style valuations. Among China’s private launch companies, Space Pioneer is the most highly valued, at roughly 22.5 billion yuan.
Government-backed money could add fresh fuel to the sector. China’s National Venture Capital Guidance Fund, established on Friday, said it would step up investment in early-stage projects and companies in areas including aerospace. Its first three regional sub-funds are each sized at more than 50 billion yuan.

data.title: 2 decoration workers remanded in custody over 1 billion yen robbery in Hong Kong
data.description:
Two men arrested over a 1 billion yen (US$6.4 million) robbery in Hong Kong were remanded in custody after appearing at Fanling Court on Friday.
Lee Wan-kwong, 36, and Lee Chun-wa, 28, were each charged with conspiracy to commit robbery alongside seven others accused of robbing two men of 1.08 billion yen on December 18 in Central.
Lee Wan-kwong was alleged to have been the mastermind behind the operation, while Lee Chun-wa was accused of handling the stolen cash.
According to court documents, both are decoration workers and hold Hong Kong identity cards.
Magistrate Kenneth Chan Ping-chau, who presided over the hearing, rejected their bail applications and ordered that the pair remain in custody.
He adjourned the case until March 17, 2026, at Eastern Court, pending further police investigation.
The seven alleged accomplices were earlier remanded in custody after a brief hearing at Eastern Court on Monday, pending further police investigations.
They are decoration workers Lee Ka-ho, 32, and Cheng Kai-hang, 30; construction worker Chan Siu-yuk, 37; and jobless defendants Choi Heung-yan, 54, Ma King-hong, 37, Ngai Sheung-ping, 40, and Ho Sin-sin, 53.
Police have so far arrested 17 suspects in connection with the case.
The robbery took place outside a currency exchange shop, during which four suitcases containing 1 billion yen, or about HK$50 million, were snatched within 30 seconds in what investigators described as a “meticulously planned” plot.
The force earlier said it was still trying to recover the cash.
The robbers targeted two employees of a Japanese company specialising in cryptocurrency and luxury goods, who had planned to exchange the money at the shop on Wing Lok Street.

data.title: Beijing sanctions 20 US defence firms over Taiwan arms sales package
data.description:
Beijing has imposed sanctions against 20 US defence firms and their executives in response to Washington’s massive arms sale package to Taiwan last week.
The sales, if approved by the US Congress, would mark the largest-ever US weapons package to Taiwan, valued at more than US$10 billion. It includes medium-range missiles, howitzers and drones.
Beijing views Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state. But Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the island by force and is legally bound to supply it with weapons to defend itself.
More to follow...

data.title: 7 of Hong Kong’s best New Year’s Eve parties to see in 2026
data.description:
From retro time-travel to rooftop glamour, Hong Kong’s New Year’s Eve parties will offer a spectacular send-off for 2025 even without the promise of fireworks at midnight.
Soak in the harbour views while clinking glasses at several of the city’s highest bars and restaurants, or embrace nostalgic flair at a Kowloon hotel’s nostalgic “Back to the Future” celebration.
A brand new harbour-facing hotel will transform its rooftop bar into a neon-lit Hollywood-themed bash, while a Kai Tak newcomer promises high-energy beats and incredible value with its free-flow package.
Whether you seek refined Japanese dining, live jazz or panoramic countdown energy, there is a place to suit your style this December 31.
1. Swim Club

Ring in 2026 with retro glamour at Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui hotel’s New Year’s Eve Hollywood Sock Hop (HK$1,288 per guest).
This vibrant rooftop party transforms the night into a scene of neon-lit nostalgia, featuring roller-skating performers, rockabilly DJ beats and breathtaking views of Victoria Harbour.
Guests can enjoy bites like tacos and sliders, a s’mores and ice cream station, and a celebratory midnight champagne toast.
50/F, Kimpton Tsim Sha Tsui, 11 Middle Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui
2. Zuma

Welcome 2026 at Zuma with a night of jazz and Japanese-themed revelry.
On offer is a refined tasting menu dinner (HK$1,680), or the premier Yashoku Night Brunch (HK$1,980 per person) – a two-and-a-half-hour feast with free-flow Ruinart champagne.
Both include access to the countdown event (regular price HK$1,080 per person), which unfolds from 10pm in a jazz-club atmosphere with live music, performances and complimentary food stations. Toast with free-flow drinks as the energy builds up to a dazzling midnight celebration.
Landmark Level 5 & 6, 15 Queen’s Road Central
3. Terrible Baby
Travel through time at Eaton HK’s terrace bar in Jordan. The Back to the Future-themed party (HK$630 per guest) transforms the venue into an immersive experience, blending retro nostalgia with a futuristic motif.
Guests will journey from past to present with live music, vibrant DJ sets and cocktails. Revel in ’80s and ’90s tracks as you bid farewell to 2025.
4/F, Eaton HK, 380 Nathan Road, Jordan
4. Summit View 58

Elevate your New Year’s Eve at Summit View 58 in the Hopewell Hotel from 9.30pm.
Enjoy panoramic skyline views alongside live band performances in a sophisticated, high-altitude setting.
The package includes unlimited prosecco, wine, beer and canapés for HK$788 per person.
Diners at Hopewell Inn or Lobby Café can join the fun for a special price of HK$488. For added indulgence, upgrade to unlimited selected champagne for an extra HK$200.
58/F, Hopewell Hotel, 15 Kennedy Road, Wan Chai
5. Avoca

Head to Avoca on the 40th floor of the Mondrian for its New Year’s Eve Countdown Party (HK$880 advance/HK$990 at the door), which kicks off at 10pm.
Usher in 2026 at the stunning bar and alfresco rooftop, where merrymakers will enjoy free-flow cocktails, sparkling wines, spirits and beers, alongside party snacks, with live DJ performances for an unforgettable night.
38/F, Avoca Bar and 40/F, Roof Garden, Mondrian Hong Kong, 8A Hart Avenue, Tsim Sha Tsui
6. Jin Bo Law

Start 2026 at Jin Bo Law’s dazzling rooftop countdown party. Soak in breathtaking views of Victoria Harbour and Kai Tak Stadium as you dance the night away to a live DJ.
The celebration includes a generous free-flow package of sparkling wine, beer, spirits and mixers. Tickets are available at a wallet-friendly price of HK$328.
43 Shing Kai Road, Kai Tak, Kowloon
7. The Terrace
Celebrate with an electrifying New Year’s Eve party at The Hari Hong Kong hotel’s terrace lounge.
From 10.30pm, enjoy live music by local artists, innovative cocktails and delectable bites.
For a complete experience, book the Festive Elegance package, including a night’s stay in a room with city views and HK$500 dining credit.
The night in the room also includes access to the party, with champagne and discounts at the hotel’s Italian and Japanese restaurants.
1/F, 330 Lockhart Rd, Wan Chai

data.title: China bolsters e-cigarette regulations to smoke out non-compliance
data.description:
China has unveiled plans to tighten controls over e-cigarette production and investment to address concerns over fierce intra-industry competition and excess capacity, the latest step in the country’s broader effort to enhance its regulatory power over tobacco, a sector which enjoys a functional monopoly.
The draft policy, released by the national tobacco regulator on Thursday, follows a State Council opinion issued earlier this month which placed e-cigarettes and nicotine pouches under stiffer oversight.
As the e-cigarette industry grapples with challenges – including the slow exit of outdated capacity, structural imbalances and poor compliance with export rules – the new regulation specifically names “involution-style” competition as an area of concern.
“Involution”, a term describing the self-defeating competition that has driven down prices and shrunk profit margins within several industries, will be limited in the tobacco sector through curbs on investment and production expansion, according to the draft document.
“No investment in new projects shall be permitted, and relocated or reconstructed facilities shall not result in any increase in production capacity.”
In principle, the measure also forbids the widening of capacity through on-site technical renovations.
Stronger oversight of production capacity was also laid out as a priority in the draft policy. Companies would be mandated to operate strictly within their approved limits, with any proposed adjustments requiring additional approval and updated licensing.


Compliant companies would, however, be allowed to merge production sites to improve their efficiency.
The policy would also impose controls on production planning, requiring companies to set annual output targets based on actual market orders to avoid a race to the bottom among firms.
The new policy also includes a crackdown on underperformance and non-compliance. Companies that fail to meet national standards on product quality and safety, or those that operate with persistently low utilisation rates or a history of regulatory violations, will be placed under heightened scrutiny and required to reduce their capacity.
Enterprises would also be expected to assume full responsibility for compliance across their entire e-cigarette export supply chain.
The draft is open for public comment until January 5.
China produces more than 90 per cent of the world’s e-cigarettes, Bloomberg reported earlier this year.
According to Beijing’s customs data, the United States is China’s largest e-cigarette export market, accounting for over 38 per cent of outbound shipments in the first 11 months of 2025.
